longTailKeywords: how long does a cold last

h1Title: Common Cold: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: The common cold — medically termed acute nasopharyngitis — is a self-limiting viral infection of the upper respiratory tract, principally involving the nasal passages, nasopharynx, and throat. Rhinovirus accounts for 30-50% of all cases and over 80% of autumn colds; the remaining cases are caused by coronavirus, adenovirus, respiratory syncytial virus, influenza, and parainfluenza viruses. Adults average 2-3 colds per year; children under 6 average 6-8 per year (CDC). Despite being the most common acute illness worldwide, no antiviral treatment has proven efficacy, and antibiotics are entirely ineffective. The economic burden exceeds $40 billion annually in the United States through productivity losses and medical visits. Symptomatic treatments including nasal decongestants, saline irrigation, and analgesics reduce discomfort while the immune system clears the infection over 7-10 days.

keyStats.prevalence: Over 1 billion cold episodes annually in the United States; adults average 2-3 per year, children under 6 average 6-8 per year (CDC)
keyStats.demographics: Children experience the highest frequency; incidence declines with age as cross-reactive immunity accumulates; no significant sex difference in frequency, though women seek care more often
keyStats.avgAge: Children under 6 years have the highest annual incidence; after age 10-15 frequency decreases; elderly adults with waning immunity experience more frequent and severe episodes
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1-2 billion cold episodes globally per year; rhinovirus alone is responsible for more hospitalizations in children during autumn than any other respiratory virus

definition: Acute nasopharyngitis (ICD-10: J00), commonly called the common cold, is a mild, self-limiting, non-specific viral infection of the upper respiratory tract affecting the nasal mucosa, nasopharynx, and often the laryngopharynx. Over 200 serotypes of virus cause the clinical syndrome, with rhinovirus (species A, B, and C), seasonal coronavirus, respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), adenovirus, parainfluenza, and human metapneumovirus being the principal agents. Pathophysiology begins when viral particles are deposited on the nasal or conjunctival epithelium through direct contact or inhalation of large respiratory droplets. Rhinovirus binds intercellular adhesion molecule-1 (ICAM-1) receptors on nasal mucosal cells and triggers a cytokine-mediated inflammatory cascade — rather than direct cellular destruction — producing vasodilation, mucosal edema, and hypersecretion. Infection is self-limiting because rhinovirus replicates optimally at 33-35°C (nasal cavity temperature) rather than at core body temperature. The condition is managed almost entirely in primary care and through self-care; referral to pulmonology or ENT is unusual and reserved for complications or when distinguishing colds from more serious conditions.

typesClassification.description: Caused by one of over 160 rhinovirus serotypes (picornavirus family). Accounts for 30-50% of all colds and over 80% of autumn colds. Transmitted primarily by hand-to-mucosa contact after touching contaminated surfaces. Incubation 1-3 days; symptoms peak at 48-72 hours; resolution within 7-10 days in most cases. No durable cross-immunity between serotypes.

Seasonal human coronaviruses cause 15-30% of common colds, predominantly in winter and spring. Generally indistinguishable from rhinovirus colds clinically. Distinct from SARS-CoV-2 but part of the same coronavirus family. Symptoms typically milder and shorter than influenza.

typesClassification.type: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) upper respiratory infection
typesClassification.description: RSV causes mild common cold symptoms in healthy older children and adults but can cause severe bronchiolitis and pneumonia in infants under 12 months and older adults. RSV cold in an adult parent or older sibling is often the source of severe RSV bronchiolitis in a young infant in the household.

Adenovirus causes cold-like symptoms but often more pronounced pharyngitis and conjunctivitis (pharyngoconjunctival fever). Transmission occurs via respiratory and fecal-oral routes. Can cause outbreaks in schools, swimming pools (from contaminated water), and military settings.

Parainfluenza types 1-4 cause colds in adults but are the primary cause of croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) in children. Type 3 is the most prevalent. Spring and autumn peaks. Often a more prolonged cold with prominent cough.

primarySymptoms: Watery nasal discharge (rhinorrhea) that typically begins clear, becomes thicker and yellow or green by days 3-5, and clears again before resolution — yellow discharge does not indicate bacterial infection and is a normal progression of viral illness.
Nasal obstruction and congestion, peaking on days 2-3, caused by mucosal edema and hypersecretion; often worse when lying down, making sleep difficult.
Sore or scratchy throat — usually the first symptom to appear within 24 hours of exposure, mild to moderate in severity, and less prominent than in streptococcal pharyngitis.
Sneezing, which is triggered by nasal mucosal irritation and is one of the principal mechanisms of viral transmission via droplets.
Mild cough resulting from postnasal drip irritating the posterior pharynx and larynx; begins around day 2-4 and may persist for 1-2 weeks after other symptoms resolve.
Malaise and fatigue proportional to the vigor of the immune response; typically mild in healthy adults, more pronounced in young children and older adults.

Low-grade fever — usually below 38.5°C in adults and older children; higher fever (up to 39°C) is more common in infants and young children and is not a reason to diagnose bacterial superinfection alone.
Headache and sinus pressure from mucosal edema and sinus ostial congestion without true sinus infection; typically mild and frontal.
Reduced sense of smell (hyposmia) from mucosal edema at the olfactory cleft — usually fully reverses within 1-2 weeks of recovery.
Mild conjunctival injection or watery eye discharge in adenoviral or rhinoviral infection; prominent conjunctivitis with pharyngitis suggests adenoviral pharyngoconjunctival fever.
earlyWarningSigns: First symptom is usually a scratchy or mildly sore throat within 12-24 hours of exposure, followed by nasal discharge onset at 24-48 hours.

Clear watery discharge suddenly becoming thick and unilateral, with facial pain worsening after initial improvement — suggests bacterial sinusitis superinfection rather than uncomplicated cold progression.
Fever persisting beyond 72 hours or worsening after day 4-5 of a cold — warrants reassessment for bacterial sinusitis, otitis media, pneumonia, or influenza.
Ear pain developing after onset of nasal congestion — suggests acute otitis media, a common bacterial complication in young children.
Marked worsening of breathing difficulty or new wheeze in a person with asthma during a cold — colds are the leading trigger of asthma exacerbations in all age groups.
emergencySigns: Rapid breathing, chest recession, and hypoxia (oxygen saturation below 92%) in an infant with cold symptoms — lower respiratory tract involvement (bronchiolitis, pneumonia) requires immediate hospital assessment.
High fever (above 39.5°C) with lethargy, inability to be consoled, rash, photophobia, or neck stiffness in a child — excludes meningitis before attributing to a cold.
Stridor (high-pitched inspiratory noise) in a young child with a cold — suggests croup or epiglottitis, both of which require emergency evaluation.
Worsening dyspnoea, productive cough with blood or very dark sputum, or pleuritic chest pain during or after a cold — possible pneumonia or pulmonary embolism in higher-risk adults.
Symptoms of severe asthma exacerbation triggered by a cold (peak flow below 50% predicted, inability to speak in full sentences) — requires emergency bronchodilator and corticosteroid therapy.

causes.cause: Rhinovirus (species A, B, C — over 160 serotypes)
causes.description: The dominant cause of common colds, responsible for 30-50% of all cases and 80% of autumn colds in temperate climates. Transmitted primarily by hand-to-mucosa contact with contaminated surfaces; aerosol transmission plays a secondary role. Replicates at 33-35°C, explaining predilection for nasal mucosa. Hundred-plus serotypes preclude lasting immunity.

Four endemic coronavirus strains cause 15-30% of colds, predominantly in winter and spring. Transmission via respiratory droplets and aerosols. Clinically indistinguishable from rhinovirus colds. Re-infection with the same strain can occur within 12-18 months as immunity wanes.

Adenovirus types 1-7 cause 5-10% of cold-like illnesses in children and adults. Notable for simultaneous pharyngitis and conjunctivitis (pharyngoconjunctival fever) and for fecal-oral transmission, enabling spread through swimming pools and contaminated surfaces. More prolonged illness than rhinovirus.

causes.cause: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) and human metapneumovirus
causes.description: RSV and hMPV cause mild cold syndromes in older children and adults but significant lower respiratory disease in infants, older adults, and immunocompromised individuals. RSV epidemics occur each autumn/winter. Annual RSV vaccines for infants (nirsevimab) and older adults are now available in several countries.

Causes 10-15% of upper respiratory infections across all ages; responsible for most cases of croup in young children. Type 3 causes year-round endemic colds; types 1 and 2 cause biennial autumn outbreaks. Adults experience mild upper respiratory symptoms while exposed children may develop croup.

causes.cause: Contact and droplet transmission in crowded settings
causes.description: Cold viruses survive on surfaces (doorknobs, phones, children's toys) for up to 8 hours (rhinovirus) to several days (adenovirus). Self-inoculation by touching the nose or eyes after hand contact with contaminated surfaces is the primary transmission route for rhinovirus. Day care centres and schools are the main amplifiers of viral spread to family contacts.

riskFactors.description: Young children average 6-8 colds per year because of immature adaptive immunity and frequent virus exposure in day care and school settings. This frequency gradually declines through childhood as cross-reactive immunity accumulates.

Children in group child care settings have 50% more respiratory infections than home-reared children (Wald et al. 1988 study). Adult parents of school-age children also have elevated cold frequency from household transmission.

riskFactors.factor: Autumn and winter seasonality in temperate climates
riskFactors.description: Cold incidence peaks October through March in the Northern Hemisphere. Cold dry air reduces mucociliary clearance efficiency; more time spent indoors facilitates respiratory droplet transmission. Rhinovirus has a separate spring season in some regions.

Cohen et al. (1991, PMID 1713648) in a landmark controlled exposure study showed that people with high perceived stress were 5.8 times more likely to develop a cold after rhinovirus inoculation. Stress-related immune suppression increases susceptibility.

Smokers have longer cold duration, more severe symptoms, and more frequent complications (bacterial sinusitis, bronchitis) than non-smokers. Smoking impairs nasal mucociliary clearance and reduces local IgA levels.

Cohen et al. (2009, PMID 19139325) showed adults sleeping fewer than 7 hours per night were 2.94 times more likely to develop a cold after rhinovirus challenge than those sleeping 8+ hours. Immune reconstitution during sleep protects against viral invasion.

Common colds are the leading trigger of acute asthma exacerbations in both children and adults, responsible for 80-85% of childhood asthma exacerbations. In COPD, rhinovirus-triggered exacerbations account for up to 40% of acute flares.

affectedDemographics: Children under 6 are the most frequently affected group, averaging 6-8 colds per year — triple the adult rate. Rhinovirus is responsible for approximately 75% of autumn colds in children (Monto 2002).
Adults aged 20-40 with school-age children at home experience higher cold frequency (3-4 per year) than their childless peers (2-3 per year) due to household transmission from children.

Adults over 65 experience fewer colds than younger adults on average due to accumulated immunity, but when infected they have more severe symptoms, longer illness, and higher risk of bacterial complications including pneumonia.

Immunocompromised patients (HIV, organ transplant recipients, those on chemotherapy) can experience prolonged rhinovirus shedding for weeks to months, atypical presentations, and severe lower respiratory involvement.
diagnosisOverview: The common cold is a clinical diagnosis based on the characteristic symptom profile — acute onset nasal discharge, nasal congestion, sore throat, and sneezing, with absent or mild systemic symptoms. Diagnostic testing is not indicated for uncomplicated colds in otherwise healthy patients. The primary clinical task is to exclude conditions that require specific treatment: influenza (onset with high fever, rigors, prominent myalgia, and rapid deterioration), streptococcal pharyngitis (exudative tonsillitis, absence of nasal congestion, positive RADT), bacterial sinusitis (symptoms beyond 10 days or double-worsening pattern), and pneumonia (fever, productive cough, reduced breath sounds). In infants and young children, assessment of respiratory rate and work of breathing is essential to exclude lower respiratory tract involvement. Point-of-care multiplex respiratory PCR panels are available and identify specific viral pathogens within hours, but are not routinely recommended for uncomplicated colds; they have value in immunocompromised patients, hospitalized patients, and in epidemiological surveillance. In children with frequent or unusually severe colds, evaluation for anatomical causes (adenoid hypertrophy), primary ciliary dyskinesia, or immunodeficiency (common variable immunodeficiency, IgA deficiency) is appropriate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms common cold and excludes streptococcal infection, influenza, bacterial sinusitis, and lower respiratory tract illness requiring specific treatment
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician asks about symptom onset, character, and duration; examines the throat, ears, and lymph nodes; and auscultates the chest. Takes 10-15 minutes. No investigations are needed in straightforward cases.

diagnosticTests.test: Rapid antigen detection test (RADT) for group A Strep
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes bacterial streptococcal pharyngitis when throat symptoms are prominent and Centor score is 2 or above
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A brief tonsillar swab providing results in 5-10 minutes. Performed selectively when pharyngitis dominates the presentation. A negative result in a typical cold presentation reassures that antibiotics are not needed.

diagnosticTests.test: Influenza rapid antigen test or PCR
diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes influenza from the common cold when clinical features suggest influenza — sudden onset, high fever, prominent myalgia, headache, dry cough — particularly in high-risk patients where antiviral therapy is appropriate
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A nasal or nasopharyngeal swab provides a result in 15-30 minutes (rapid test) or 2-6 hours (PCR). Sensitivity of rapid tests is 60-80% for influenza A; PCR is over 95%. Used when treatment decision depends on the result.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the specific viral pathogen causing upper respiratory illness; used in immunocompromised patients, hospitalized patients, and outbreak investigations
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A nasopharyngeal swab collected by a healthcare worker. Sent to laboratory; results available in 4-8 hours for rapid panels. Detects rhinovirus, RSV, influenza, coronavirus, adenovirus, and other respiratory viruses simultaneously.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for lower respiratory tract involvement and hypoxia in infants, young children, older adults, and those with underlying lung disease during a cold
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clip placed on the fingertip or infant's toe for 30 seconds. Painless. Oxygen saturation below 92% on room air indicates significant respiratory compromise and warrants further assessment.

treatmentOverview: No antiviral treatment is available for the common cold; management is entirely symptomatic and supportive. The priority is relieving discomfort, maintaining hydration, and preventing transmission. Analgesics and antipyretics (ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily or paracetamol 1 g four times daily) reduce throat pain, headache, and fever; ibuprofen shows marginally superior analgesia for sore throat in RCTs. Nasal decongestants — oral pseudoephedrine (60 mg every 4-6 hours for adults) or topical oxymetazoline 0.05% (restricted to 3 days maximum) — reduce nasal obstruction through alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction. Nasal saline irrigation reduces mucosal edema and clears secretions. Antihistamines have modest benefit for sneezing and rhinorrhea; first-generation agents (chlorphenamine) have additional anticholinergic effects that dry secretions but cause sedation; second-generation antihistamines have minimal effect on cold symptoms. Zinc acetate lozenges (13.3 mg elemental zinc every 2-3 hours within 24 hours of symptom onset) reduce cold duration by approximately 1 day in two systematic reviews; zinc nasal spray should be avoided due to documented reports of permanent anosmia. High-dose vitamin C supplementation (above 1 g/day) taken at symptom onset does not significantly shorten cold duration in the general population but reduces severity by approximately 8% in meta-analyses. Antibiotics are not indicated and should not be prescribed for uncomplicated viral colds. First-generation antihistamine-decongestant combinations should be avoided in children under 6 years due to lack of efficacy and risk of adverse effects. Honey (15 mL) modestly reduces cough and discomfort in children over 12 months. Rest and adequate hydration are the best-supported general measures.

medicalTreatments.name: Ibuprofen (400 mg every 6-8 hours) or paracetamol (1 g every 4-6 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Analgesics and antipyretics for sore throat, headache, and fever. Ibuprofen provides superior analgesia for pharyngeal pain compared with paracetamol in RCTs. Appropriate for adults and children at weight-based doses; ibuprofen should be avoided in infants under 3 months and those with renal impairment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces pain by 30-45% vs placebo; ibuprofen superior to paracetamol for throat pain by 0.5-1 point on 11-point scale in RCTs

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal saline irrigation (isotonic or hypertonic, 240 mL twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: High-volume nasal saline irrigation reduces mucosal edema, clears secretions and viral particles, and maintains ciliary function. Evidence supports use in both adults and children. Neti pot or squeeze bottle devices are effective.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces nasal symptom scores by 25-40% and reduces antibiotic use in systematic reviews of upper respiratory infections

medicalTreatments.name: Oral pseudoephedrine (60 mg every 4-6 hours for adults)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line oral decongestant for nasal obstruction. Alpha-adrenergic agonist that reduces mucosal blood flow and edema. Restricted in patients with hypertension, cardiac disease, hyperthyroidism, and in pregnancy. Available over the counter in the US but subject to pharmacy verification.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest but statistically significant reduction in nasal blockage vs placebo; effects last 4-6 hours

medicalTreatments.name: Topical oxymetazoline 0.05% (2 sprays per nostril twice daily, maximum 3 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Provides rapid nasal decongestion within 10-15 minutes. Restricted to 3 days maximum to prevent rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound congestion). Effective for short-term relief of nasal obstruction in adults and children over 6 years.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective nasal decongestion onset within 15 min; no evidence of benefit beyond symptom relief; rebound congestion with use beyond 3 days

medicalTreatments.name: Zinc acetate lozenges (13.3 mg elemental zinc every 2-3 hours from symptom onset)
medicalTreatments.description: Zinc ions inhibit rhinovirus replication and reduce release of histamine from mast cells. Must be started within 24 hours of first symptom and continued at 2-hourly intervals while awake. Zinc nasal spray should not be used due to anosmia risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces cold duration by approximately 1 day (1.03 days in Cochrane meta-analysis by Hemila and Chalker 2015); reduces severity scores

medicalTreatments.name: Honey (15-25 mL in warm water or herbal tea)
medicalTreatments.description: Modest evidence from RCTs supports honey for reducing nocturnal cough and improving sleep in children over 12 months with upper respiratory infections. Cohen et al. 2012 showed honey superior to placebo for cough in children. Not to be given to infants under 12 months (botulism risk).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces cough frequency and severity scores vs no treatment or placebo in pediatric RCTs; evidence in adults is limited

alternativeTreatments.name: Echinacea (standardized extract)
alternativeTreatments.description: A Cochrane review (Karsch-Volk et al. 2015, PMID 25629583) found modest evidence that some Echinacea preparations reduce cold incidence by 10-20% and duration by up to 1.4 days; effect sizes are small and preparation-dependent. Well-tolerated; considered a low-risk option.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin C supplementation (1-2 g/day from symptom onset)
alternativeTreatments.description: High-dose vitamin C started at symptom onset does not significantly shorten cold duration in the general population but reduces severity by about 8% in Cochrane meta-analyses (Hemila and Chalker). Therapeutic dose in marathon runners reduces cold incidence by 50%, suggesting benefit in physically stressed populations.

whySeeSpecialist: Most colds do not require specialist care. See a doctor if symptoms worsen significantly after initial improvement (bacterial sinusitis), ear pain develops (acute otitis media), fever persists beyond 5 days, breathing becomes difficult, or signs of pneumonia appear. An ENT is appropriate if colds seem more frequent than expected or recurrent bacterial sinusitis develops. Pulmonology referral is appropriate for patients with asthma experiencing frequent cold-triggered exacerbations.
doctorSelectionGuide: A primary care physician or general practitioner handles all straightforward cold presentations. Pharmacists can advise on over-the-counter symptomatic remedies. For parents concerned about frequency in young children, a pediatrician can assess for immunodeficiency or adenoid hypertrophy. For cold-triggered asthma exacerbations, a pulmonologist or allergist optimizes preventive inhaler therapy.
hospitalCriteria: Infants under 3 months with fever — requires evaluation to exclude bacterial sepsis regardless of apparent cold symptoms
Oxygen saturation below 92% on room air indicating lower respiratory tract involvement (bronchiolitis, pneumonia)
Severe asthma exacerbation triggered by a cold not responding to initial bronchodilator therapy
Dehydration in infants or young children unable to maintain fluid intake due to nasal obstruction or lethargy
Immunocompromised adults with prolonged febrile respiratory illness from rhinovirus or other respiratory virus requiring antiviral evaluation
keyFacilities: Primary care clinics — the appropriate first point of contact for all cold presentations
Community pharmacies — for over-the-counter symptomatic treatments and advice
Pediatric emergency departments for infants with respiratory distress
ENT outpatient clinics for recurrent cold-related complications (sinusitis, otitis media, adenoid hypertrophy)
preventionStrategies: Wash hands with soap and water for at least 20 seconds or use alcohol-based hand sanitizer frequently, especially after touching shared surfaces in public places — the primary intervention for reducing rhinovirus transmission.
Avoid touching the eyes, nose, and mouth with unwashed hands — hand-to-mucosa self-inoculation is the dominant transmission route for rhinovirus.
Maintain adequate sleep (7-9 hours for adults; age-appropriate amounts for children) — sleep deprivation nearly triples rhinovirus susceptibility in controlled exposure studies.
Annual influenza vaccination prevents influenza, which produces cold-like symptoms in its early stages and causes severe complications.
Avoid smoking and passive smoke exposure — smokers have significantly more frequent, severe, and prolonged colds than non-smokers.
Regular moderate exercise (150 minutes/week) is associated with 40-50% fewer upper respiratory infections compared with sedentary behavior in observational studies.
lifestyleModifications: Rest and avoid strenuous physical activity while febrile or in the first 2-3 days of symptoms to allow immune focus on viral clearance.
Increase fluid intake to at least 2 L/day — hydration keeps mucus secretions thin and aids mucociliary clearance.
Use a humidifier in the bedroom (40-50% relative humidity) to prevent mucosal drying and optimize mucociliary function.
Avoid alcohol during acute infection — alcohol impairs immune function and dehydrates.
Elevate the head of the bed when sleeping to reduce nasal congestion, snoring, and postnasal drip.
For people with asthma: increase peak flow monitoring frequency during colds and start a short-acting bronchodilator preemptively as directed by your asthma action plan.

recommended: Warm fluids — chicken broth (demonstrated in one RCT to enhance nasal mucociliary clearance compared with hot or cold water), herbal tea, and warm water with honey and lemon
Hot spicy foods with chili or horseradish — capsaicin acts as a natural decongestant, transiently improving airflow
Foods rich in vitamin C (citrus, kiwi, peppers, berries) — while high-dose C supplements have modest evidence, dietary vitamin C supports immune baseline
Zinc-rich foods (oysters, pumpkin seeds, beef, cashews) in the early days of a cold to support the antiviral mechanisms suggested by zinc lozenge trials
avoid: Alcohol — impairs innate immune response and worsens dehydration
Dairy products — while evidence for mucus-thickening is weak, some individuals report subjectively worse congestion; reduce if symptomatic relief occurs
Sugary processed foods that may blunt acute immune responses
Caffeinated beverages in excess — mild diuretic effect can worsen dehydration; moderate intake is acceptable

exerciseGuidelines: The 'neck rule' widely used by sports medicine practitioners suggests light exercise (walking, yoga) is acceptable if all symptoms are above the neck (runny nose, sore throat, sneezing, no systemic symptoms). Rest is advised when fever is present, when symptoms extend below the neck (chest congestion, myalgia, fatigue), or when feeling significantly unwell. Return to full training when asymptomatic for 24 hours. Vigorous exercise during a cold does not accelerate recovery and may increase complication risk in high-intensity training.
dailyManagement: Irrigate nasal passages with saline morning and night using a squeeze bottle or Neti pot to maintain drainage and remove viral particles.
Use analgesics on a schedule rather than waiting for pain to peak — consistent dosing maintains more stable symptom control.
Limit topical decongestant sprays to 3 consecutive days maximum to prevent rebound congestion.
Increase room humidification during dry winter months — ideal indoor humidity is 40-50%.
Monitor for complications: fever worsening after day 4-5, ear pain, worsening facial pressure, new cough or shortness of breath — any of these warrants medical assessment.
Stay home from work or school during the first 2-3 days of symptoms (the most infectious period) to reduce transmission to others.
prognosis: The common cold is self-limiting in essentially all immunocompetent individuals. Symptoms peak at 48-72 hours and resolve in 7-10 days in over 90% of healthy adults. Cough can persist for up to 3 weeks after nasal symptoms clear, which is a normal post-viral phenomenon rather than a sign of bacterial complication. Children tend to have more severe and prolonged symptoms than adults. Bacterial complications — acute otitis media in children (complicating 5-15% of colds in under-5s), bacterial sinusitis (0.5-2% of colds), and secondary pneumonia — increase morbidity. Colds trigger asthma exacerbations in 80-85% of school-age asthmatic children. In immunocompromised patients, rhinovirus and RSV can cause prolonged pneumonia and respiratory failure. RSV in infants under 3 months causes bronchiolitis requiring hospitalization in approximately 1-2% of infections.
recoveryTimeline: Incubation: 1-3 days. Symptom onset to peak: 1-3 days. Peak to partial recovery: days 5-7. Full recovery: 7-10 days in most adults. Cough may persist 2-3 weeks post-cold (post-viral cough). Children under 6: 7-14 days typical. Elderly or immunocompromised: up to 2-3 weeks.
complications: Acute otitis media — the most common complication in children under 5, occurring in 5-15% of cold episodes; caused by bacterial spread along the Eustachian tube during mucosal inflammation; presents as ear pain developing 3-7 days into a cold.
Bacterial acute sinusitis — develops in 0.5-2% of viral colds through ostial obstruction and bacterial superinfection; diagnosed by symptoms persisting beyond 10 days or double-worsening pattern.

Acute asthma exacerbation — rhinovirus-triggered exacerbations account for 80-85% of childhood asthma flares and are the leading cause of asthma-related hospitalization; intensive inhaler therapy guided by action plans reduces severity.

Lower respiratory tract extension — viral tracheitis, bronchitis, and pneumonia complicate a small proportion of colds, particularly in infants (RSV bronchiolitis), elderly adults, and immunocompromised patients.
Acute exacerbation of COPD — rhinovirus triggers 40% of COPD exacerbations requiring hospitalization; annual influenza vaccination and pneumococcal vaccination reduce exacerbation frequency.

supportResources.name: CDC — Common Colds: Protect Yourself and Others
supportResources.description: CDC guidance on cold symptoms, prevention, and when to seek medical care, including advice for parents of young children.

supportResources.name: NHS — Common Cold
supportResources.description: UK NHS patient information on cold symptoms, self-treatment, and when to see a GP, including advice on whether antibiotics are needed.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Pediatrics — Colds in Children
supportResources.description: AAP guidance for parents on managing colds in children, including safe use of over-the-counter remedies and warning signs.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Pharyngitis (Strep Throat)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Streptococcal pharyngitis presents with sudden severe sore throat, tonsillar exudate, high fever, and tender neck glands, without rhinorrhea or cough. The common cold begins gradually with nasal symptoms, sneezing, and mild sore throat — not sudden severe throat pain. A rapid strep test distinguishes them; positive result warrants antibiotics, negative does not.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Sinusitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sinusitis is suspected when cold symptoms persist beyond 10 days without improvement, worsen after initial improvement, or include severe facial pain with purulent unilateral discharge. Common cold produces bilateral clear-to-yellow secretions that improve by day 7-10 without facial pain or tenderness on sinus palpation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Bronchitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute bronchitis presents with a productive cough lasting 2-3 weeks as the primary complaint, often with normal nasal symptoms. The common cold has prominent nasal symptoms with cough as a secondary feature. Both are usually viral; neither requires antibiotics in healthy adults, but the clinical emphasis differs.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute Tonsillitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tonsillitis involves prominent tonsillar enlargement and exudate with systemic features; nasal symptoms are minimal. The common cold features rhinorrhea and sneezing as dominant complaints with mild throat involvement. Examination of the tonsils and a strep test distinguish them.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Sinusitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Bacterial sinusitis complicates 0.5-2% of common colds through ostial obstruction; distinguishing uncomplicated cold from sinusitis guides antibiotic prescribing.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Pharyngitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Mild sore throat accompanies most colds; prominent pharyngitis with exudate and fever requires a strep test to exclude bacterial pharyngitis.

relatedConditions.name: Acute Bronchitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Post-viral cough from a cold can progress to acute bronchitis; both are viral in most cases and both are managed symptomatically without antibiotics.

relatedConditions.name: Croup
relatedConditions.relevance: Parainfluenza virus — a common cold cause in adults — triggers croup (laryngotracheobronchitis) in young children; parents may present with a cold while the child develops stridor from the same viral exposure.

faqs.question: How long does a common cold last?
faqs.answer: A common cold typically lasts 7-10 days in healthy adults. Nasal symptoms peak at 48-72 hours and then gradually improve. Cough can persist for up to 3 weeks after other symptoms resolve — this is a normal post-viral phenomenon. Children under 6 may have symptoms lasting up to 2 weeks.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a cold and the flu?
faqs.answer: Influenza begins suddenly with high fever, severe muscle aches, headache, and pronounced fatigue — out of proportion to nasal symptoms. A cold starts gradually with runny nose, sneezing, and mild sore throat; fever and myalgia are absent or mild. A rapid flu test can confirm influenza when the distinction matters for treatment.

faqs.question: Should I take antibiotics for a cold?
faqs.answer: No. All common colds are caused by viruses, and antibiotics are entirely ineffective against viruses. Taking antibiotics for a cold provides no benefit, causes side effects including diarrhoea and allergic reactions, and contributes to antibiotic resistance. Symptomatic treatments are the only appropriate management.

faqs.question: What stops a cold fastest?
faqs.answer: No treatment cures the cold, but several shorten it or reduce severity. Zinc acetate lozenges (13.3 mg every 2-3 hours) started within 24 hours of symptom onset reduce duration by about 1 day. Ibuprofen controls pain and fever. Nasal saline irrigation and decongestants improve congestion. Rest and hydration support recovery.

faqs.question: Does vitamin C cure the common cold?
faqs.answer: Vitamin C does not prevent colds or significantly shorten them in the general population. A Cochrane review found it reduces cold severity by about 8% when taken continuously as a supplement. It may reduce incidence in people under physical stress such as marathon runners. Starting high-dose C after symptom onset has minimal effect.

faqs.question: How long am I contagious with a cold?
faqs.answer: You are most contagious in the first 2-3 days when viral shedding peaks. Contagiousness typically continues until symptoms resolve, up to 7-10 days. Hand washing and avoiding face touching are the most effective ways to prevent spreading the virus. Staying home for the first 2-3 days reduces transmission risk significantly.

faqs.question: Is yellow or green snot a sign of bacterial infection?
faqs.answer: No. Yellow or green nasal discharge is a normal stage of viral cold progression on days 3-5 and does not indicate bacterial infection. It results from neutrophils and cellular debris entering the mucus as the immune response peaks. Antibiotics are not needed based on discharge color alone.

faqs.question: Does cold weather cause colds?
faqs.answer: No — cold viruses, not cold weather, cause colds. Cold weather increases indoor crowding, which facilitates transmission. Rhinovirus replicates at 33-35°C (nasal temperature) rather than core body temperature, meaning cold air does not directly cause infection. Controlled exposure studies confirm that chilling does not increase susceptibility if virus is absent.

faqs.question: Is it safe to exercise with a cold?
faqs.answer: Light exercise (walking, gentle yoga) is generally safe when symptoms are confined to the nose and throat with no fever — the 'neck rule' used in sports medicine. Rest is advised if fever is present, you feel systemically unwell, or chest symptoms develop. Vigorous training during a cold does not accelerate recovery.

faqs.question: What is the best medicine for a cold?
faqs.answer: No single medicine cures a cold. Ibuprofen or paracetamol relieves pain and fever. Nasal saline irrigation and pseudoephedrine reduce congestion. Zinc acetate lozenges may shorten duration by about one day. Honey reduces cough in children. Antihistamines (first-generation) dry secretions but cause drowsiness. Use based on which symptoms bother you most.

faqs.question: Can I give cold medicine to my child?
faqs.answer: Over-the-counter cold medicines are not recommended for children under 6 years — combination decongestant-antihistamine products lack efficacy and carry safety risks in this age group. For infants, nasal saline drops and suctioning are safe. For children over 6, some products are approved at age-appropriate doses. Honey (not under 12 months) and paracetamol or ibuprofen can be used at weight-based doses.

faqs.question: Does stress make you more likely to catch a cold?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cohen et al. (1991) showed people with high perceived stress were nearly 6 times more likely to develop a cold after controlled rhinovirus exposure. Chronic stress raises cortisol, which suppresses innate antiviral immune responses. Sleep deprivation also triples cold susceptibility in controlled studies.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for a cold?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if fever lasts more than 5 days or returns after improving, symptoms worsen significantly after day 5, ear pain develops, breathing becomes difficult, or you have a medical condition like asthma, COPD, or immunosuppression. Infants under 3 months with any fever should be seen same-day.

faqs.question: How many colds is too many in a year?
faqs.answer: Adults averaging more than 6 colds per year or experiencing unusually severe or prolonged episodes warrant investigation for underlying immunodeficiency (common variable immunodeficiency, IgA deficiency) or anatomical factors such as adenoid hypertrophy. Children average 6-8 per year normally, so frequency alone is less alarming in children before age 10.

faqs.question: Can a cold turn into pneumonia?
faqs.answer: Rarely, in healthy adults. Viral colds can occasionally extend to the lower respiratory tract, and secondary bacterial pneumonia from Streptococcus pneumoniae or Staphylococcus aureus complicates a small proportion of influenza infections. The risk is higher in older adults, infants, smokers, and immunocompromised patients. Worsening chest symptoms or persistent fever after day 5 warrant reassessment.
